First, we want to acknowledge the seriousness of dog attacks
and the lasting impact they have on victims,
families and communities.
We also acknowledge the petitioner's commitment
to improving public safety and victim support.
We'd like to highlight three points from our submission.
Firstly, councils under the Dog Control Act 1996
operate within strict legal boundaries.
Dogs are legally considered property
and euthanasia of a dog generally requires a court order.
Every decision to prosecute following a dog attack must meet
evidentiary standards
and follow the solicitor general's pro
prosecution guidelines.
For example, in the Y Macari case,
the council applied the legal requirements under the act.
The council classified the dog as dangerous under section 31
and under section 32 the dog was required to be muted
with conditions imposed on the owner
that removed any further risk to the public.
As a result, the dog has not reoffended.
Secondly, we do not support a mandatory national dog bite
scale under the Dog Control Act 1996.
While tools like the Dunbar Scale can help
mandating one risks limiting enforcement discretion,
councils need the ability
to investigate dog attacks on a case by case basis.
And this allows councils to
take into consideration broad factors such
as the dog's history, compliance, record,
and community risk.
And thirdly, the DO Control Act is nearly 30 years old.
The current system is generally fit for purpose,
but we believe a review would be beneficial.
We support a sector-wide consultation
before any legislative changes.
We do not support penalties for councils.
These could undermine discretion
and lead to risk averse enforcement
instead will be back DIA's approach
of strengthening community consultation for bylaws.
Looking ahead, we recently attended the launch
of the New Zealand Animal Law Association's report on dog
attacks, which opens a valuable con conversation
about updating the act.
DIA has already started reviewing enforcement guidelines
and we've helped circulate their survey
to animal control managers
to ensure wide engagement across the sector.
In short, public safety matters,
enforcement must remain fair
and legally sound collaboration is key
to any future changes.

source_metadata.description:
Petition of Melinda McGregor: Councils need clear guidelines of required actions following a dog attack

Taituarā | Local Government Professionals NZ

Raymond Horan, Chief Advisor
Philip Shackleton, Principal Advisor
Billy Charlton, Environmental Services Manager, Waimakariri District Council
Lionel Bridger, Manager Animal Services, Christchurch City Council
